results.title: Stanford dropouts raise nearly $10M to launch AI dating app – NBC Bay Area
highlights:
Stanford dropouts raise nearly $10M to launch AI dating app – NBC Bay Area
[...]
# Stanford dropouts raise nearly $10M to launch AI-driven dating app
[...]
## The new app, called Known, uses an interactive chatbot to build personality profiles and gives users a strict 24-hour window to agree to a face-to-face date.
[...]
Two former Stanford University students have raised nearly $10 million for a new artificial intelligence-driven dating app designed to eliminate endless scrolling in favor of immediate, real-world dates.
[...]
The startup app, called Known, founded by Celeste Amadon and Asher Allen, relies on conversational AI rather than traditional profile building.
[...]
Upon signing up, users do not fill out standard text prompts or check boxes. Instead, they have a voice-based interview with an AI matchmaker that maps out personalities, preferences, values, likes and dislikes.
[...]
Once the algorithm determines it has gathered enough information to predict compatibility, it pairs the user with a single curated match.
[...]
The app then imposes a strict timeline: users have 24 hours to accept the match and agree to meet for an actual, face-to-face date.
[...]
“Known is now open to the greater Bay Area. All the way from Sausalito down to Saratoga, we’re going to be setting up dates for not just San Franciscans, but everyone who is in the Bay. We’re really excited to help more people find love,” she added.

results.title: New AI-Run Dating App Based In SF Claims Half Its Matches Say ‘Yes’ to Real Dates
highlights:
New AI-Run Dating App Based In SF Claims Half Its Matches Say ‘Yes’ to Real Dates
[...]
Known, a new voice-based AI dating app that launched in San Francisco in February, was founded by two 22-year-old Stanford dropouts who say it results in more dates than traditional apps.
[...]
Co-founders and former Stanford students, Celeste Amadon and Asher Allen, who raised $10 million in funding for the app, say that Known replaces profiles, swiping, and in-app messaging with voice-based AI matchmaking, as KTVU reports. Users instead go through an onboarding interview where they talk through their background, personality, values, and what they’re looking for in a partner, with the app using those conversations to determine compatibility.
[...]
The company says its matching model analyzes conversational details and personal preferences to pair users with a single potential match at a time, while also handling introductions and helping arrange dates. According to Fast Company, the app is designed around getting people to meet in person, rather than maximizing engagement and retention like subscription-driven dating apps.
[...]
Known also claims that about half of its matches result in users agreeing to a real-life date, which Amadon contrasted with the far lower meet-up rates associated with conventional dating apps.
[...]
The app’s early testing phase included about 7,000 participants in a beta round last year, with the founders reportedly saying they’ve already heard from some users who met through the platform and are still dating.
[...]
After each date, Known follows up with users for feedback, which Amadon says helps refine future matches by improving the system’s understanding of compatibility over time, per KTVU. Photos are withheld until later in the matching process, once a connection has already been established.
[...]
If both users agree to meet, Known coordinates logistics such as time and location, aiming to reduce the back-and-forth and drop-off that’s common on other apps. The platform is free to join, but users pay a $15 fee when they commit to a match, a charge the company says is meant to discourage no-shows.
[...]
Amadon tells KTVU the app currently has thousands of users in San Francisco following its launch in February.
[...]
Additionally, Fast Company reports that dating apps across the industry are incorporating AI features, including Tinder, Hinge, and Facebook Dating.
[...]
Back in 2023, SFist reported on a dating app that aimed to get people meeting offline as soon as possible, which went defunct less than a year later.

results.title: Thursday, the dating app that you can use only on Thursdays, expands to San Francisco | TechCrunch
results.author: Lauren Forristal
highlights:
Thursday, the dating app that you can use only on Thursdays, expands to San Francisco | TechCrunch
[...]
# Thursday, the dating app that you can use only on Thursdays, expands to San Francisco
[...]
11:58 AM PDT · August 8, 2024
[...]
Thursday seeks to shake up conventional online dating in a crowded market. The app, which recently expanded to San Francisco, fosters intentional dating by restricting user access to Thursdays. At midnight, all matches disappear. The idea is that by restricting usage to one day a week, potential matches will be encouraged to set up real-life meetings sooner.
[...]
Many singles, especially younger users, are dumping traditional dating apps due to “swiping fatigue,” a term used to describe the feeling of exhaustion that comes from swiping through countless profiles. This, along with other negative experiences, such as suffering from burnout after talking to too many people, or getting stuck in an in-app messaging trap that rarely results in an in-person date, has caused some younger users to tire of traditional dating apps. For instance, Tinder, which essentially invented the swipe-left/swipe-right approach to dating, has lost paid users for seven consecutive quarters.
[...]
In contrast, Thursday encourages users to use the app when they genuinely want to date. By promoting in-person meetups sooner rather than later and deleting matches after 24 hours, the app aims to prevent users from endless scrolling and seeking validation from dozens of matches they’ll never interact with. In fact, Thursday lets you match with only 10 people a day, unless users pay a $19/month subscription fee to remove the cap.
[...]
Additionally, the company hosts exclusive IRL (in real life) singles events through a separate app called Thursday Events. The app offers large meetups at venues like bars, running clubs, gym classes, dance studios and art galleries. Users can also apply to organize their own events.
[...]
Thursday’s most recent launch in San Francisco comes as dating giants Bumble and Match Group (owner of Tinder and Hinge) grapple with the challenges of adjusting to the post-pandemic dating landscape and people’s general frustration with dating apps.
[...]
Match also experienced setbacks in Q2, including a 6% reduction of its workforce after ending its livestreaming services in dating apps Plenty of Fish and BLK. Notably, Tinder experienced a slump in paid users for the seventh quarter in a row.
[...]
Other dating startups have also tried to capitalize on disillusionment with Bumble, Tinder and Hinge. Over the years, we’ve seen various new apps emerge, catering to all kinds of user behavior and communities. This includes gamer-focused platforms, an app that hides selfies until users message each other, an app that matches users based on favorite dating spots, and so on. There’s even a dating app for people with good credit scores.
[...]
Thursday launched in 2021 and was founded by Matthew McNeill Love and George Rawlings. To date, it has 906,000 total global downloads across iOS and Android devices, according to estimates from app intelligence provider Sensor Tower.
[...]
The app is currently available in six markets — Australia, Canada (Toronto only), Ireland, the U.K., the U.S. and Sweden (Stockholm only) — in 26 cities, including Austin, Texas; Dublin, Ireland; Chicago, Illinois; London, England; Miami, Florida; New York, New York; Sydney, Australia; and more.
[...]
The company’s goal is to be in 100 cities by the end of 2024.

results.title: Bumble’s AI assistant Bee will learn what you want from a relationship
results.author: Allison Steffens Herrera
highlights:
Bumble’s AI assistant Bee will learn what you want from a relationship
[...]
The dating app unveiled Bee at its Q4 earnings alongside a broader ‘Bumble 2.0’ overhaul, as the company attempts to reverse years of declining user numbers by replacing gamified swiping with AI-driven compatibility.
[...]
There is a version of the future, Bumble’s version, at least, where you never swipe on a dating app again. Instead, you have a private conversation with an AI that learns what you actually want from a relationship, sits quietly in the background, and surfaces one carefully chosen match with a note explaining exactly why the two of you belong together.
[...]
That is the pitch behind Bee, the AI dating assistant Bumble reveled at its fourth-quarter earnings on 11 March 2026.
[...]
The product is still in internal testing, with a public beta coming “soon” according to founder and chief executive Whitney Wolfe Herd, who described it to investors as a personal matchmaker that learns users’ values, relationship goals, communication style, lifestyle, and dating intentions through private chats.
[...]
Once Bee identifies two people it considers compatible, both are notified in the app with a summary of why they make a strong match. From there, the conversation and the date are up to the humans.
[...]
The launch vehicle for Bee is a new in-app experience called Dates. Users opt in, complete an onboarding conversation with Bee via text or voice, and then wait for a curated introduction rather than scrolling through an open pool of profiles.
[...]
Bumble says future applications of Bee will extend to date-night suggestions based on shared interests, and an optional anonymous feedback loop from previous matches to help the system and the user understand what went wrong.
[...]
Bee is the headline act in a broader product reset, Bumble is calling Bumble 2.0. Alongside the AI assistant, the company is experimenting with removing the traditional left-right swipe mechanic entirely in select markets, replacing it with what it calls chapter-based profiles, structured layouts that let users showcase different dimensions of their lives, from work and hobbies to values and plans.
[...]
The urgency behind Bumble 2.0 is not hard
[...]
read. Full-year 2025 revenue fell 10% to $966 million, paid users declined 11.5%, and the Q4 quarter alone saw revenue drop nearly 15% year on year to $224.2 million.
[...]
JPMorgan upgraded BMBL from Underweight to Neutral, citing stabilising leading indicators and the Bumble 2.0 launch, targeted for Q2 2026, as a potential catalyst. Wells Fargo kept an Equal Weight rating but pointed to Q1 EBITDA guidance of $80 million, roughly 42% above analyst estimates, as a sign that margin discipline is taking hold.
[...]
Bumble is not alone in the AI pivot. Tinder’s Chemistry feature uses personal questions and camera roll access to sharpen match recommendations. Grindr’s Edge subscription tier offers AI summaries of past chats and compatibility statistics.
[...]
What Bee represents, if it works, is a more ambitious commitment: not augmenting the swipe, but replacing it as the primary discovery mechanism.
[...]
That is a significant trust ask. Users interacting with Bee will be sharing detailed, intimate information about what they want from relationships with an AI system, and implicitly allowing that system to make consequential social decisions on their behalf.
[...]
The chapter-based profiles and potential no-swipe markets are scheduled for the second half of 2026, according to the product timeline Wolfe Herd shared with investors.
[...]
Bee’s beta will arrive before that. For a company that has spent most of the past two years shrinking, both in ambition and headcount, it is a notably bold sequence of bets all riding on the idea that what daters actually want is less choice, not more.

results.title: Taking Online Dates Offline: Dating App Breeze Launches in the U.S., Starting with NYC
highlights:
Taking Online Dates Offline: Dating App Breeze Launches in the U.S., Starting with NYC
[...]
Taking Online Dates Offline: Dating App Breeze Launches in the U.S., Starting with NYC
[...]
The successful European platform aims to bring singles together IRL in the city with one of the highest amounts of dating app users in the world.
[...]
NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- Breeze, the dating app for real dates, announces its expansion into the United States. Starting with New York City, the platform targets online dating fatigue by encouraging users to schedule an in-person date right away without endless swiping and chatting.
[...]
With established success in Europe since its launch in 2020, Breeze has organized over 300,000 dates across the Netherlands, Belgium, Germany, the U.K., and France, with partner locations in more than 34 cities. Designed for all singles, the platform has also focused on establishing itself as an inclusive option for the LGBTQ+ community.
[...]
- No Chatting: Unlike other dating apps, Breeze does not have a chat function, meaning users take time back from hours of endless swiping. With limited curated potential daily matches, users immediately share their availability and schedule their date after matching at one of the Breeze-partnered bars and restaurants.
[...]
- Honest Business Model: Breeze does not sell subscriptions or user data for advertising. Instead, users only pay once they receive value: a date. This means the company's goals are in line with theirs: Getting them off the app and on a date as quickly as possible.
[...]
- Designed for Safety: All daters are screened and urged to verify their identity. First dates are hosted in safe and secure partner locations where the staff can keep an eye out for you.
[...]
- Intentional Dates: Breeze daters show their commitment to meeting each other in-person by paying up front. Users' accounts get frozen if they cancel too often. No-shows rarely happen, but if they do, users get banned from the app.
[...]
- Trending Partner Locations: Dates are hosted at nearby, verified venues with a discounted drink included in the reservation made in-app. A few notable NYC locations include: Romeos, The Seville, Juliette Restaurant, and more!
[...]
”We’re so excited to introduce New Yorkers to a new way of online dating by focusing on what we all crave—safe and face-to-face dates based on real chemistry,” said Marco van der Woude, Co-Founder & Head of Business Development of Breeze. “The average dating app user spends around 38 hours swiping and chatting before they end up meeting someone in real life, and it's clearly not working. We’re redefining the experience by eliminating pre-date messaging altogether, prioritizing face-to-face interactions.”
[...]
Breeze was founded in 2020 by seven student co-founders in the Netherlands with a mission to make online dating human again. Frustrated by the inefficiency of traditional dating apps, they set out to create a platform that prioritizes quality over quantity, making dating effortless and intentional. Breeze eliminates endless scrolling–removing the digital barriers that keep people from meeting in real life– focusing instead on effortless, real-life dates. Since its launch, Breeze has expanded across the Netherlands, Belgium, Germany, the U.K., France, and now the U.S., helping singles find genuine connection beyond the screen. Learn more at breeze.social.

results.title: Breeze: Can a Dutch app save Brits from swiping past our dismal dating scene? | The Independent
highlights:
The Dutch couple are one of many success stories spawning from the dating app Breeze, which launched in Europe in 2020 before landing in London in 2025. Breeze promises date nights without endless swiping, and banishes the talking stage (what’s your favourite colour? How many siblings do you have?”) by only opening the chat window two hours before the date. When you match with someone, you put your availability into the app and it books you both a table at one of its partner bars. You each pay £9.50 for a “date token” that covers your first drink. Anyone who cancels is frozen out of the app for a week.
[...]
Breeze was launched by a group of university students as the answer to modern dating’s most irking issues: ghosting, becoming pen pals, and endless options (the app only sends you a limited number of potential matches each day at 7pm). In the Netherlands, where it’s the fastest-growing and third most popular dating app, behind Bumble and Tinder, it’s evidently working – but can it save Britain’s dismal dating scene?
[...]
“I’m just looking for it in a different way than most, which may be to my detriment but I’ve been enjoying it nonetheless,” he says. “If I wanted to be long-term, it would 100 per cent be with the girls I’ve been on Breeze dates with,” he adds, noting the women he’s met have been more “charming” and “lovely” than on other apps. “ Hinge is a mixed bag,” he says.
[...]
The fundamental selling point of Breeze is that it gets you out the door. Ruben had already matched with Liv multiple times on Tinder before he met her through Breeze, unaware that his potential future wife was lost in his pile of unmet matches. “I couldn’t figure out where I knew her from,” he says. “When I looked in my phone I even had her number already.”
[...]
London dating that people cancel because
[...]
t be bothered,”
[...]
has been spreading
[...]
Still, even the biggest Breeze enthusiast concede that there are some issues with the app. Principally, its choice of locations which seem to be slightly worse in the UK than in Western Europe. While Ruben and Liv met at a “really cute wine bar” near the historic harbour in Rotterdam, Mark suffered through his first three initial Breeze dates spent at the same “very odd” Chelsea bar that “felt like a Sixties sex dungeon”. “Not my vibe,” he says, adding the app sends multiple other first-date couples to the same location, which leaves you feeling like you’re in the First Dates restaurant, though the eavesdropping is great.
[...]
There are certain things Breeze can save Brits from: boring convos generated by ChatGPT? Yes. Being strung along for weeks with no actual date follow-through? Yes. Last-minute cancellations? Less likely but, ultimately, no. And what it absolutely can’t save the nation from is ourselves. Riding the rejection roller coaster of the dating landscape is undeniably knackering – and if fatigue starts to be felt, then the best remedy is to give it all a rest.
[...]
But Becky admits Breeze has a fundamentally good design, if you can shake off your apathy. So far, the platform has organised 752,208 dates, with 19 of them leading to the couple starting a family. “It’s definitely better than constantly swiping on Tinder or Hinge,” she says. “It felt way more intentional. Which I really liked… But the dates themselves just made me realise that I’m not really bothered by dating at all at the moment. Still, it’s a nice option. There are always freaks out there – but I’d be up for trying it again when I’m in the right frame of mind.”

results.title: Dutch Dating App Breeze Launches in the UK With Offline-First Model - Global Dating Insights
results.author: Staff Writer
highlights:
Dutch Dating App Breeze Launches in the UK With Offline-First Model - Global Dating Insights
[...]
Dutch dating app Breeze has officially launched in the UK, positioning itself as an alternative to swipe-heavy platforms by encouraging users to move quickly from matching to meeting in person. The rollout is supported by a new brand campaign that directly challenges what Breeze describes as the industry’s overreliance on extended in-app conversations.
[...]
The UK launch is accompanied by a national advertising push titled Love Is Worth The Leap, developed by creative agency Cloudfactory. The campaign centers on the anxiety many users feel before meeting a match face to face, reframing that hesitation as a necessary step toward meaningful connection. In the lead television spot, a woman receives a date notification that visually transforms her bedroom into a high diving platform, symbolizing the emotional leap involved in meeting someone without prolonged online chatting.
[...]
Breeze’s approach differs from most mainstream dating apps by limiting pre-date messaging. Instead of extended conversations, users match and are prompted to meet directly at a public venue arranged through the app. The company argues this reduces ghosting, endless swiping, and mismatched expectations that can develop through text-based interactions.
[...]
Sarah Fronckevic, chief marketing officer at Breeze, said: “Dating apps have become endless chat machines that keep people stuck behind screens. We built Breeze to do just the opposite. You skip the chat and go on a real date. We invite Breeze users to step out of their comfort zone, because real connection is worth it. We’re proud to work with Cloudfactory to bring this insight to life as we roll out our service across the UK.”

results.title: Breeze: World’s First Dating App to Organise Offline Dates at Scale - My Framer Site
highlights:
: World’s First Dating App to Organise Offline Dates at Scale
[...]
My Framer Site
[...]
# Breeze: World’s First Dating App to Organise Offline Dates at Scale
[...]
Breeze: World’s First Dating App to Organise Offline Dates at Scale
[...]
: World’s First Dating App to Organise Offline Dates at Scale
[...]
: World’s First Dating App to Organise Offline Dates at Scale
[...]
##### Founded in 2019 by former TU Delft students Marsha Goei, Marco van der Woude, Thomas van der Pas, Daan Alkemade, Thomas Oomens, Philip van den Heuvel, and Joris van Doorninck, Breeze is a dating app that streamlines the process of organising quick, safe, real-world meetings, reducing the need for prolonged online chatting.
[...]
##### After a successful pitch on the Dutch version of Dragons’ Den in 2021, Breeze raised €1.3M in 2022 and expanded to Belgium in 2023. Last month, the company expanded further to London. We spoke to the founders about the company’s plans moving forward.
[...]
We had two initial ideas related to dating: a ‘swipe-for-your-friends dating app’ and a ‘blind-date Tinder that arranges the date’. We found that people were frustrated with current apps, particularly during the chatting phase. Research showed that people spend an average of 38 hours on Tinder per date. Realising the potential to improve the dating experience, we decided to drop out of our Master’s programmes and commit to building a dating app focused on facilitating offline dates.
[...]
Unlike most dating apps that follow the ‘swipe, match, chat’ concept popularised by Tinder, Breeze operates on a ‘match, plan, date’ model. When you match on Breeze, there’s no chat. Instead, users fill in a date picker. Based on their availability and addresses, we make a reservation at one of our partnered hospitality venues. Users pay for our matching service, arrangement, the reservation, and the first drink provided by the venue.
[...]
Whilst we have a smart venue selection and reservation system, our main technological focus is on matchmaking. We send users a limited number of suggested matches twice daily (at 7am and 7pm). This means we don’t just filter and sort available profiles; we use complex recommendation systems to optimise both locally and globally, sending the best number and set of profiles for all users.
[...]
In essence, we use technology not to optimise for engagement and retention through endless swiping and chatting, but to facilitate actual matches and offline dates.
[...]
A key challenge we faced was Covid-19. We had just launched the app in early 2020 and had great traction with national media, but just one month later, the pandemic hit. Where other dating apps thrived, our model was (and is) organising offline dates at partnered bars. For a long time the bars were closed and people were reluctant to meet new people.
[...]
So we didn’t earn any money – all our active users churned because we had no service to deliver. We were unsure if we would continue or pause for a while to work or finish studies. It was a very close call. We’re happy we stuck together – we found a temporary solution by organising ‘Walk & Talk’ dates and eventually raised a small round during those uncertain times from friends and family.
[...]
Several Rise alumni recommended the programme to us, and our recent launch in Germany and plans for the UK and US expansions made it the perfect time to join.
[...]
Our goal is to have organised 5 million dates by the end of 2026. We aim to become one of the biggest dating apps in the major cities in the EU and US. If you want to keep track of our progress, the current amount of dates organised is on our website.
[...]
#### You expanded to the UK in May by launching in London.
[...]
How has it been so far?The launch in London has been very successful – we have seen that there is quite a lot of ‘dating app fatigue’ in London, especially among women. We have outpaced our goals there so far and continue to grow weekly with double-digit numbers. We already plan to launch in one or two other cities in the UK.

results.title: Breeze Sees Growing But Gradual Success After London Expansion - Global Dating Insights
results.author: Staff Writer
highlights:
Breeze, a Dutch dating app that launched in Europe in 2020 and expanded to London in 2025, is gaining traction by focusing on structured in-person meetings rather than prolonged online interactions. The app limits daily matches and automatically schedules dates at partner venues, aiming to bypass the often exhausting “talking stage.” Users pay a £9.50 “date token” for their first drink, and those who cancel are temporarily banned from the platform.
[...]
The platform’s design directly addresses common frustrations with swipe-heavy platforms, including ghosting, endless messaging, and decision fatigue from too many options. Chat only opens two hours before the scheduled date to encourage quicker offline meetings.
[...]
The platform has organized over 752,000 dates globally – and while user feedback is mixed, due to the reliance on the partner venues (which the platform has limited influence over), Breeze has become the third most popular dating app in the Netherlands, behind Bumble and Tinder.
[...]
Despite these issues, Breeze seems to be seeing great success entirely because of its in-person focus and its design that discourages things like AI conversations, stringing along matches and unexpectedly sudden date cancellations. Given that more and more singles are turning to thoughtful and careful approaches to dating, platforms like Breeze seem clearly primed to draw in a large audience that have been burned by other dating platforms in the past.

Thursday, May 14, 2026

SAN FRANCISCO (KGO) -- Artificial intelligence is moving into the dating world, with Bumble announcing a new tool designed to help users find matches more effectively.

The company said it is introducing an AI assistant called Bee as part of its matchmaking process. Bumble said the feature aims to give users a more personal way to connect on the platform.

The company has also announced it will remove the swipe -- a key feature of the app for users to express interest in potential matches. Swiping right meant you were interested and left meant you were not.

If two users swiped right on each other, they were matched and could start a conversation.

With the update, members will be able to share more detailed information about themselves beyond basic profile details.

The company said that additional information will allow the AI assistant to suggest matches more closely aligned with users' hobbies and interests.

The update also includes a notable shift in how conversations start on the platform. Women will no longer be required to send the first message, marking a change from Bumble's previous format.

The company said the additions reflect an effort to evolve the app's approach to helping people connect.

results.title: Airbnb-backed WeRoad raises $58M to take its group travel platform to the US | TechCrunch
results.author: Lauren Forristal
highlights:
Airbnb-backed WeRoad raises $58M to take its group travel platform to the US | TechCrunch
[...]
-backed We
[...]
raises $5
[...]
to the US
[...]
WeRoad, the Milan-based group travel startup, has raised a $58 million Series C round led by Airbnb as it prepares for its first major expansion outside Europe. The funding brings the company’s total capital raised to roughly $100 million and will finance WeRoad’s push into the U.S., beginning with Austin.
[...]
The new investment reflects a bet that the next generation of travel companies may look less like booking platforms and more like social platforms designed to facilitate real-world connections.
[...]
The U.S. launch also arrives as loneliness, particularly among younger consumers, has become both a public health concern and an emerging business opportunity. When much of the tech industry remains focused on AI, WeRoad is positioning itself as part of the growing “IRL economy,” a category of startups monetizing offline interaction rather than screen time. Companies like Timeleft, 222, and Pie are pursuing similar ideas through dinners, clubs, events, and community-based experiences.
[...]
“It started from a very personal need. When you finish college and start working, it becomes harder to find people to travel with. Friends were settling down, having kids, moving away, or simply couldn’t align schedules anymore,” De Nadai told TechCrunch. “My co-founder Fabio and I both tried companies offering similar group travel experiences for solo travelers, but while the trips were good, something was missing. The guides were professional local experts, and the groups were mixed in age, and people didn’t really see eye to eye. People were traveling together, but not really connecting.”
[...]
The founders’ response was to redesign group travel around shared interests. WeRoad trips are primarily designed for younger travelers and grouped around shared interests and travel styles. Customers can book trips through the platform based on themes such as beach vacations or skiing.
[...]
“We asked ourselves, ‘What if we created trips for Millennials and Gen Z travelers, bringing together people from the same age groups with shared cultural references but completely different backgrounds, and focused on creating real bonds between them?’” De Nadai added.
[...]
Before each trip begins, travelers are added to a WhatsApp group managed by the group leader so members can begin getting to know one another ahead of time. Groups typically include between eight and 15 travelers.
[...]
“The biggest concern people have is rarely the destination,” De Nadai said, but usually concerns that they won’t connect with the group. To address that, WeRoad intentionally structures itineraries around social dynamics. More adventurous or collaborative activities are often scheduled early in the trip to help break the ice.
[...]
Additionally, instead of traditional tour guides, WeRoad has “group leaders,” coordinators closer in age to travelers who act more like travel companions. The company now works with more than 4,000 group leaders globally.
[...]
“We’re not looking for destination experts, but for people with travel experience and strong soft skills. Can they lead a group, handle tension, adapt when plans change, and help strangers connect?” De Nadai said.
[...]
WeRoad has also begun expanding beyond travel itself. In 2025, the company launched WeMeet, an app focused on local in-person gatherings, including dinners, hikes, yoga classes, running groups, after-work drinks, and board game nights. WeRoad says more than 50,000 people attended WeMeet events across 35 cities last year, while the app reached 150,000 downloads.
[...]
The company says WeMeet will also play a central role in its U.S. expansion strategy. Rather than immediately scaling nationwide, WeRoad plans to focus on a small number of cities first, beginning with Austin, where it will recruit group leaders, organize local events, and build community partnerships before expanding further.
[...]
“We’ll be launching WeMeet events across multiple U.S. cities throughout 2026, starting with Austin because of its incredible energy and vibrant community scene,” De Nadai said.
[...]
WeRoad says it generated €130 million in revenue in 2025, up 30% year over year, while taking more than 100,000 travelers on trips last year alone. Since launching in 2017, the company says it has organized travel for more than 300,000 customers across over 1,000 itineraries globally.

results.title: WeRoad raises $58M and expands to the US with Airbnb | WeRoad

WeRoad raises $58M and expands to the US with Airbnb | WeRoad

# WeRoad closes a $58 million Series C round. And now, we’re heading to the United States
[...]
WeRoad closes a €58 million round to accelerate international growth and launch in the US. The Italian travel-tech company aims to scale its group travel model for Millennials and Gen Z globally.
[...]
- $58 million Series C round closed by WeRoad, led by Airbnb and joined by existing investors, including H14.
- A total of $100 million raised since the company was founded.
- The funding will support WeRoad’s first expansion outside Europe: entering the U.S. market.

- WeMeet in 2025: 2,000 events, 50,000 participants, 35 cities, and 150,000 app downloads.

WeRoad has closed a $58 million Series C funding round led by Airbnb. The round also included our long-time investors, including H14. The total funding raised since the company was founded has now reached $100 million, which will support WeRoad’s first major expansion outside Europe: the United States.

WeRoad started in 2017. Since then more than 300,000 people have traveled with us across over 1,000 itineraries around the world. In the last year alone, 100,000 people traveled with WeRoad and around 90% of them joined solo — and almost none of them returned feeling that way.

Behind all of this is a community built step by step from 3.5 million social media followers to more than 4,000 Group Leaders. Our Group Leaders are experienced travelers who lead each group, manage logistics, and, most importantly, are the reason a group of strangers no longer feels like strangers by the end of the trip.

In 2025, we realized that the desire to connect in real life wasn’t limited to people traveling for two weeks on the other side of the world. It also applied to people living in the same city who, maybe on a Tuesday evening, feel like going out for dinner, going on a hike, joining a yoga session, or enjoying an evening drink with new people.
[...]
That’s how WeMeet was born: an offline experiences platform open to everyone, including people who have never traveled with us. In one year we’ve held 2,000 events, with 50,000 participants, in more than 38 cities, and 150,000 app downloads. Numbers that confirmed something we already suspected — people aren’t just looking for new places. They’re looking for people.
[...]
We live in a time when artificial intelligence and social media are reshaping the way we connect with each other. And amid all this digital connection, real human connection has become increasingly rare. Around 30% of young adults say they feel lonely every day. In the United States, this phenomenon is especially significant.
[...]
In the United States, we’re launching with WeRoad itineraries already available in the international market, alongside a selection of itineraries designed specifically for the American market, as well as WeMeet events, which will serve as the first point of contact with the local community. Recently, we’ve just held our first selection weekend for US Group Leaders in Austin. It’s a beginning!
[...]
Entering the United States doesn’t change the way we work in Europe: our trips, Group Leaders, and WeMeet events will continue as always. But it does open a new chapter in a market where demand for shared experiences is growing faster than anywhere else — and where we believe we can bring something meaningful.

New in town? Or just eager to meet new people and try something different? Then WeMeet might be exactly what you’re looking for. It’s not about dating, swiping, or small…

results.title: Airbnb Leads $58 Million WeRoad Funding for U.S. Expansion
results.author: Dennis Schaal

Airbnb Leads $58 Million WeRoad Funding for U.S. Expansion

Airbnb has struggled to scale experiences. Investing in WeRoad — and hiring its CEO — is Chesky's preferred move: partner first, potentially acquire later.

Airbnb led a $58 million Series C funding round for European tour and experience platform WeRoad Wednesday — a day after announcing it had hired its CEO to lead Airbnb hotels.

With the deal and partnership, Airbnb gets a 10% stake in WeRoad and a board seat alongside its new VP of hotels, Andrea D’Amico. In addition to serving as WeRoad CEO since 2022, D’Amico worked as a top Booking.com hotel executive in EMEA for 18 years.

WeRoad, which operates multiday tours and has a platform for tour guides operating experiences, could help Airbnb scale its experiences business.
[...]
One of Airbnb’s biggest challenges in trying to scale its experiences business has been attracting quality tour guides to run them in a way that’s compatible with the Airbnb brand. There are no publicly laid out plans yet for Airbnb to tap into WeRoad’s tour guide network, to learn from them, or even to train them, but it’s clear that WeRoad wants to make the most of this marriage.

We are a great couple, in a way,”

Road founder and executive

De Nadai

Skift. “So on our board
[...]
be one guy from Airbnb, and also Andrea as a bridge. It will be easier for us to know each other and build more synergies.”
[...]
. “But

WeRoad, which was founded in Italy in 2017, plans to use the proceeds from the funding to expand into the U.S. De Nadai said the company will hire a U.S. general manager, adding that he sees U.S. expansion as easier than expanding in some European markets like Portugal where the company would have to start from scratch.
[...]
WeRoad serves as a tour operator in popular destinations, and distributes tours from other vendors in less popular markets, De Nadai said. Its WeMeet app enables the WeRoad community to self-organize everything from a group trip to a Japanese restaurant to a Yoga lesson.

It’s the producers of those more organized experiences that might be attractive to Airbnb in helping to scale its experiences business.

De Nadai said synergies with Airbnb haven’t been discussed by the two companies yet, but he’s obviously open to

With the Airbnb-led round, WeRoad has raised around $100 million to date. WeRoad has not yet named a successor to D’Amico.

Home Funding Airbnb leads €49 million Series C in Italian adventure travel company WeRoad
[...]
# Airbnb leads €49 million Series C in Italian adventure travel company WeRoad
[...]
WeRoad, the Italian adventure travel company, has closed a €49 million ($58 million) Series C round in order to fund their first major expansion beyond Europe, bringing its community-driven travel model to the US market.
[...]
The round was led by Airbnb, with existing investors, including 2023’s Series B lead investor H14, also participating. The round brings the total amount raised by the company to €85 million ($100 million).
[...]
Paolo De Nadai, Founder of WeRoad, said: “In a world increasingly shaped by AI and social media, genuine human connection is becoming both rarer and more valuable. At WeRoad, we’ve built our entire product around enabling real-world connections through shared travel experiences. People today are not just looking to visit new places, they’re looking to belong.”
[...]
Founded in 2017, WeRoad is now reportedly one of the fastest-growing travel operators in Europe.
[...]
With the strength of its 4,000+ Coordinators, WeRoad has facilitated over 300,000 travellers to discover the world in more than 1000 itineraries. Around 60% of its customers rebook year-on-year and a third of its bookings come from word of mouth.
[...]
WeRoad says their main appeal is as a pioneer of the ‘IRL economy’. The company outlines that millennials and Gen Z, its core markets, are hungry for the kind of connections that can only be made offline, and increasingly willing to spend to find them.
[...]
The IRL economy, which uses tech to facilitate real-world experiences rather than drive long-term online engagement, is above all a response to widespread loneliness. Studies show that young people are among the groups most affected by the loneliness epidemic, and roughly 30% of young adults report feeling lonely every day.
[...]
In 2025, WeRoad expanded its community model beyond travel by launching WeMeet: a platform for local, curated social gatherings, including day hikes, after-work drinks & dinner, sporting events, and yoga sessions that are open to anyone, not just WeRoad travelers.
[...]
In 2025 alone, more than 50,000 people across 35 cities attended 2,000 WeMeet events, and the official WeMeet app was downloaded 150,000 times.
[...]
WeMeet events will be central to WeRoad’s US rollout. The company will lead with its digital platform and online marketing capabilities, using targeted campaigns and strategic partnerships to identify and build its strongest markets.
[...]
From there, WeRoad will deploy the full community playbook it has refined across Europe and build a homegrown network of American coordinators, establishing local partnerships and rolling out events and offline marketing activities across multiple cities.
[...]
In parallel with the Series C and US expansion, WeRoad CEO Andrea D’Amico, who has a long career at Booking.com, will relocate to San Francisco to lead hotels at Airbnb. He will remain closely involved with WeRoad as a Board member and Paolo De Nadai, founder of WeRoad, will continue to lead the company supported by co-founders Fabio Bin, WeRoad’s CMO, and Erika De Santi, Chief of Experience, along with the broader leadership team and new international hires as the company enters the US market.

results.title: WeRoad raises $58M led by Airbnb to fuel US expansion of its travel community platform | Vestbee
results.author: Joy Laoun
highlights:
WeRoad raises $58M led by Airbnb to fuel US expansion of its travel community platform | Vestbee
[...]
# WeRoad raises $58M led by Airbnb to fuel US expansion of its travel community platform
[...]
Milan-based firm WeRoad, which builds small-group adventure travel and local social experiences through its community-driven platform, has raised$58 million led by Airbnb with support from existing investors, including H14.
[...]
- Founded in 2017 by Paolo De Nadai, Fabio Bin, and Erika De Santi, WeRoad focuses on building small-group adventure trips and local social experiences that turn travel into a community-driven activity rather than an individual service.
[...]
- It operates across multiple European markets, including Italy, Spain, the UK, France, and Germany, and extends its model through “WeMeet,” a platform for organizing in-person social events.
[...]
- The company has served over 300,000 travelers across more than 1,000 itineraries, supported by a network of roughly 4,000 coordinators who lead groups and manage the on-the-ground experience. Its trips typically bring together small groups of around 8 to 15 people, designed to encourage social interaction among mostly solo travelers of similar age.
[...]
- Additionally, WeRoad expanded into local experiences through WeMeet, which in 2025 hosted over 2,000 events with around 50,000 participants across 35 cities, extending its model from multi-day trips to everyday social connection.
[...]
- With the fresh capital, the firm aims to accelerate its expansion into the United States by launching its group travel itineraries and WeMeet social experiences in key cities, while building a local network of coordinators, strengthening partnerships, and investing in marketing to scale its community-driven model internationally.
[...]
"Entering the United States doesn’t change the way we work in Europe: our trips, Group Leaders, and WeMeet events will continue as always. But it does open a new chapter in a market where demand for shared experiences is growing faster than anywhere else and where we believe we can bring something meaningful. It’s the biggest step we’ve taken so far. And in some ways, it’s still just the beginning," explains WeRoad's team.

results.title:
What WeRoad's $58M Series C Signals for Social Group Adventure Travel
 – New Market Pitch
highlights:
A strategic Airbnb-led round turns WeRoad from a European group travel scale-up into a test case for whether structured travel can become offline social infrastructure.
[...]
On May 27, 2026, WeRoad announced a $58M Series C led by Airbnb, taking total funding to about $100M. The money supports WeRoad's first major expansion outside Europe, with a U.S. launch built around existing and U.S.-specific itineraries, WeMeet local events, and U.S. Group Leader recruitment starting in Austin. The company says it has reached 300,000 lifetime travelers, including 100,000 in the last year, supported by 4,000 coordinators, 3.5M followers, and more than 1,000 itineraries.
[...]
The thesis is not simply that travel demand is back. The sharper thesis is that structured group travel is becoming a consumer social product, not just a travel booking product. WeRoad is betting that younger travelers, especially Millennials and Gen Z, are buying belonging, age-matched groups, social safety, and real-world connection alongside the destination itself. WeMeet makes that thesis more interesting because its 2,000 events, 50,000 participants, and 150,000 app downloads give WeRoad a local community wedge before the customer buys a trip.
[...]
WeRoad is now the best-funded disclosed startup-style company among its direct social group adventure travel competitors, with total funding reported at about $100M after the Series C. Among comparable disclosed peers, Much Better Adventures has about $14M in total funding and Flash Pack has about $9.49M.
[...]
The round history also shows cheque-size acceleration. WeRoad raised €13.5M in 2021, €18M in 2023, and $58M in 2026. The time between the 2021 financing and the 2023 Series B was about 25.5 months. The time between the 2023 Series B and the 2026 Series C was about 30 months. The company is not raising more frequently. It is raising much larger rounds when it does raise.
[...]
Current funding activity in community-led group adventure travel is thin in deal count but extremely concentrated in capital. Over the last 24 months, three category-relevant disclosed rounds stand out: WeRoad’s $58M Series C, WanderOn’s ₹54 crore, roughly $6.5M, Series A, and Much Better Adventures’ £5M, roughly $6.37M, growth investment.
[...]
The capital picture is far more dramatic than the deal-count picture. Over the last 12 months, the category raised about $64.5M, driven by WeRoad’s $58M and WanderOn’s roughly $6.5M. Over the last 24 months, the category raised about $70.9M after adding Much Better Adventures’ roughly $6.37M.
[...]
WeRoad captured about 81.8% of all disclosed category capital over the last 24 months. That makes it the clear number one by capital received. WanderOn captured about 9.2%, and Much Better Adventures captured about 9.0%. In practical terms, WeRoad is not just leading the category. It is almost the whole visible funding market.
[...]
In conclusion, it is very clear that WeRoad is the main investable proxy for this category right now. The optimistic interpretation is that WeRoad is early to a category that will expand as younger consumers choose shared experiences, trust, and social belonging over generic travel booking. The main risk is that the category remains too operationally complex and too niche for many venture-backed winners.
[...]
The thesis behind WeRoad’s Series C is strong because it is backed by company traction, category capital concentration, and a broader consumer shift toward offline social products. The core thesis is simple and compelling: structured group travel is becoming a consumer social product, not just a travel booking product.
[...]
WanderOn,
[...]
WanderOn is the closest recent comparable. It raised ₹54 crore, roughly $6.5M, in a Series A on January 20, 2026. Its thesis is community-led, experience-first travel for Gen Z and Millennial travelers, with capital going toward global expansion, new experience-led formats, technology, and community. That is very close to WeRoad’s logic: younger customers, curated travel, group experience, and community beyond the trip.
[...]
Across the strict travel-thesis set, about $70.9M was raised over the last 24 months. WeRoad contributed $58M of that total, or about 81.8%. That gives WeRoad the number one rank in the thesis set by a massive margin. WanderOn and Much Better Adventures validate the thesis, but WeRoad owns the capital signal.
[...]
Sweatpals raised a $12M seed round in October 2025 for local wellness events and social fitness communities. The same pattern appears again: fitness is the wrapper, but belonging is the product. For WeRoad, travel plays the role that fitness plays for Sweatpals. It gives people a reason to show up, meet strangers, and form community.
[...]
The strongest evidence for the thesis is still company-specific. WeRoad has 300,000 lifetime travelers, 100,000 travelers in the last year, a 60% rebooking rate, one-third of new customers from word of mouth, 4,000 coordinators, and WeMeet metrics of 2,000 events, 50,000 participants, and 150,000 downloads. Those numbers show a company building both travel supply and community demand.
[...]
hard part is that the
[...]
In conclusion, it is very clear that WeRoad’s Series C is one of the more interesting consumer travel rounds right now because it reframes travel as offline social infrastructure. The company has enough traction to make the thesis credible, enough capital to pressure the category, and enough strategic validation to matter. The U.S. launch will determine whether WeRoad becomes a true category creator or remains a very well-funded European group travel operator.
[...]
The retained investment thesis behind WeRoad's Series C is that structured group travel is becoming a consumer social product, not just a travel booking product. This thesis was retained because the round is explicitly tied to U.S. expansion, WeMeet local events, group leader recruitment, and WeRoad's reported traction across travelers, rebooking, word of mouth, coordinators, followers, itineraries, and event participation.
[...]
The category used for market-activity analysis is social group adventure travel. It includes companies that package multi-day or short-format consumer trips, organize travelers into small groups, use coordinators or guides, and make social connection part of the value proposition. It excludes generic online travel agencies, hotel and flight booking platforms, single-activity marketplaces, corporate travel tools, pure creator-trip infrastructure unless the company owns the traveler-facing group experience, and dating apps unless travel is central.
[...]
The direct competitor set used for funding comparisons includes Flash Pack, EF Ultimate Break, Contiki, G Adventures' 18-to-Thirtysomethings and Roamies products, Intrepid Travel's youth and small-group adventure products, and Much Better Adventures. Airbnb Experiences, GetYourGuide, Viator, and Klook are excluded from the direct competitor set because they are primarily activity or experiences marketplaces rather than age-banded, multi-day social group travel operators. Competitor funding rankings include only private or venture-backed companies with comparable disclosed financing data, so larger operators, public-company divisions, and mature brands without comparable round data are discussed qualitatively but excluded from startup-style funding rankings.
[...]
The similar-thesis set includes companies whose round narrative is more than 80% aligned with WeRoad's retained thesis. The retained travel peer rounds are WeRoad's $58M Series C, WanderOn's ₹54 crore Series A, roughly $6.5M, and Much Better Adventures' £5M growth investment, roughly $6.37M. Flash Pack is not included in the 24-month similar-thesis window because its last reported funding was in October 2023. Klook is not included because an experiences marketplace thesis is too broad and not strongly centered on strangers becoming a community.

results.title: Airbnb Social Network: What the WeRoad Deal and Hotels VP Hire Reveal
results.author: Uvika Wahi
highlights:
Airbnb announced a$58 million Series C investment in Italian adventure-travel startup WeRoad, taking a 10% equity stake and a board seat. Concurrently, Airbnb hired WeRoad CEO Andrea D’Amico to serve as its new Vice President of Hotels, replacing Jesse Stein.
[...]
On the surface, these moves address two separate corporate objectives: capturing the Millennial and Gen Z group travel market, and recruiting an 18-year Booking.com veteran to aggressively scale hotel supply.
[...]
However, analysing the transaction through the lens of Airbnb’s stated corporate strategy reveals a unified direction. CEO Brian Chesky has repeatedly positioned Airbnb as an antidote to modern loneliness. The WeRoad investment and D’Amico’s appointment demonstrate that Airbnb is now deploying capital to acquire the infrastructure required to build a real-life (IRL) social network.
[...]
historically favors organic
[...]
or light partnerships — such
[...]
with Timeleft and Welcome Pick
[...]
— to expand its non-accommodation categories. Taking a
[...]
10% equity position in a specialised tour operator indicates a structural shift.
[...]
community mechanics.
[...]
WeRoad sells curated group trips to Millennials and Gen Z travellers who do not want to travel alone — and who, more importantly, want to come home with friends. The headline metric is this: 60% of WeRoad customers rebook.
[...]
WeRoad’s 60% rebook rate isn’t a vacation metric — it’s a community metric. That’s the social network infrastructure Airbnb’s $58M is buying into.
[...]
The $58M will fund WeRoad’s U.S. launch, starting in Austin. That gives Airbnb direct access to a social-first traveller base inside its biggest domestic market.
[...]
D’Amico’s resume addresses two critical
[...]
needs. First, his
[...]
18 years at Booking.com, culminating as Vice President and Managing Director for EMEA, provides the operational playbook required to scale hotel supply rapidly. Airbnb is moving past internal product experiments and installing seasoned OTA executives to secure market share.
[...]
But D’Amico also just spent years running a company whose core skill is engineering friendships among strangers. He is not arriving at Airbnb as a hotel executive who happens to have social-travel experience. He is arriving as a social-travel executive who happens to have hotel experience.
[...]
Boutique hotels in Airbnb’s world are not going to be treated like Booking’s hotels. They will be treated as nodes in a lifestyle platform. Places where the trip happens, but also where the community forms. Hotels as supply, yes. Hotels as social venues, also yes. This is consistent with what Airbnb’s hotel pilot has been quietly testing since 2025 in New York, Los Angeles, and Madrid.
[...]
Airbnb’s Connections and Past Trips tabs already exist inside every guest profile. The Airbnb social network infrastructure isn’t coming — it’s already there. WeRoad just gives it the supply it needs to scale.
[...]
Now add WeRoad. A WeRoad trip produces multiple verified, high-trust connections between real people who actually spent a week together. Drop those people back into Airbnb, and Airbnb gets:
[...]
- PriceLabs
- Verified offline relationships
- Shared taste data
- Repeat group-travel intent
- Identity continuity across trips
[...]
This is what an in-real-life social graph actually needs. You cannot build it from booking data alone. You can build it from booking data plus group-trip data. That is what Airbnb just bought. The Connections feature was always the foundation — WeRoad is the accelerant.
[...]
If Airbnb is going to reward group dynamics, social-first travellers, and IRL connection, then the algorithm will eventually reward the listings that match. Airbnb’s 800-signal ranking model already evaluates listings far beyond price and availability — communal fit and experience quality are increasingly part of that signal set.
[...]
WeRoad’s 60% rebook rate is not about the destinations. It is about the structure: group cooking classes, guided hikes, organised dinners, a wine tour with a real guide. This is what Millennial and Gen Z group travellers are paying for, and it is what Airbnb is now subsidising on its own platform. Airbnb Experiences’ relaunch in 2025 was the first signal. WeRoad is the confirmation.
[...]
D’Amico’s appointment means boutique hotels are about to get serious attention on Airbnb. If you operate in markets with strong boutique hotel competition, expect the platform to surface them more aggressively. Distinguish on the things hotels structurally cannot offer: full kitchens, communal living, group capacity, and, increasingly, your ability to host the kind of trip people come home from with new friends.
[...]
The WeRoad investment is small in dollar terms. The strategic signal is not small at all. Airbnb is no longer dressing up its loneliness narrative as a slide in an investor deck. It is now buying the supply, hiring the executives, and writing the checks needed to make a real-life social network actually work.
[...]
Property managers who treat their listings as “places to sleep” will keep winning some bookings. Property managers who treat their listings as “places where groups become friends” will be the ones the algorithm starts pushing.

results.title: Airbnb Backs WeRoad To Deepen Group Travel And Community Ties - Simply Wall St News
highlights:
- Airbnb led a $58 million Series C funding round for European group travel startup WeRoad.
- The deal supports WeRoad's planned U.S. launch and expansion of community-focused, in-person events.
- The move extends Airbnb's reach beyond home rentals into group travel and offline travel communities.
[...]
Airbnb, traded as NasdaqGS:ABNB, is backing WeRoad at a time when its own stock is trading at $134.5. Over the past 3 years, the stock is up 19.9%, while the 5 year return shows a decline of 6.7%. These mixed longer term figures give investors a reference point as Airbnb explores adjacent travel segments such as group experiences and community based trips.
[...]
This investment positions Airbnb more directly in group travel, a segment that connects online platforms with offline social experiences. For investors tracking its broader travel ecosystem, the WeRoad deal adds another piece to how the company may seek to deepen engagement with travelers beyond core short term stays.
[...]
For you as an investor, Airbnb leading WeRoad’s US$58m Series C round looks like a targeted way to extend its “whole trip” ambitions into group travel without building a new product from scratch. WeRoad’s focus on hosted group trips and in person community events sits close to Airbnb’s push into experiences and services, so this backing can be seen as an attempt to stay closer to younger, experience seeking travelers who often plan trips around social activities, not just accommodation. Compared with larger online travel competitors such as Booking Holdings, Expedia Group and Trip.com, this move leans more toward community and offline meetups, which could support engagement even when guests are not actively booking stays on Airbnb.
[...]
- The WeRoad partnership lines up with the narrative that cross selling experiences and services can increase usage per customer and deepen earnings diversification as more of a trip’s spending flows through platforms tied to Airbnb.
- Funding a group travel startup also adds execution risk to new verticals that analysts already view as early stage, which could weigh on returns if community heavy formats scale more slowly than expected.
- The specific angle of curated group trips and local meetups is not fully reflected in the current focus on long term stays, remote work and international expansion, so investors may want to watch whether management starts highlighting group travel as a separate pillar.
[...]
- ⚠️ Airbnb is putting more weight on newer travel formats, so if group travel demand or community events soften, the return on this capital could end up below expectations.
- ⚠️ Deeper involvement in curated trips exposes Airbnb more directly to operational, regulatory and liability issues around hosted tours and group experiences, on top of existing short term rental scrutiny.
- 🎁 If WeRoad’s US launch gains traction, Airbnb could get an early look at group travel trends and traveler behavior, which may inform product decisions across its broader platform.
- 🎁 The partnership provides another route to increase engagement without relying solely on discounting or heavy marketing, which can be helpful alongside efforts to improve monetization and booking efficiency.
[...]
From here, you may want to track how often Airbnb references WeRoad in future product updates, whether any integration appears in the Airbnb app, and how quickly WeRoad expands beyond its initial U.S. city launch. Signals around booking volumes, repeat trips and cross usage between WeRoad groups and Airbnb stays could show whether this partnership helps Airbnb deepen traveler relationships or remains a financial investment. Competitive reactions from Booking Holdings, Expedia Group and other travel platforms in group tours and community led travel will also help you judge how differentiated this move really is.
[...]
Operates a platform for stays, experiences, and services worldwide.

results.title: WeRoad Secures $58M Investment from Airbnb to Improve Its U.S. Group Travel Platform - Tech Weekly
results.author: Lauren Schultz
highlights:
WeRoad Secures $58M Investment from Airbnb to Improve Its U.S. Group Travel Platform - Tech Weekly
[...]
WeRoad, a Milan-based startup focused on group travel, has secured an impressive $58 million in a Series C funding round led by Airbnb. This marks their initial significant venture outside Europe. The raised funds, totaling nearly $100 million, will facilitate WeRoad’s expansion into the U.S. market, beginning with Austin.
[...]
This investment signals a growing trend that travel companies may transition into social platforms designed to foster authentic offline connections, diverging from the conventional booking websites.
[...]
As WeRoad moves forward with its U.S. expansion, it caters to increasing concerns about loneliness among younger generations. This issue has emerged as both a critical public health concern and a practical business opportunity. While numerous tech firms are shifting towards AI, WeRoad is embracing the burgeoning “IRL economy,” which emphasizes offline interactions over digital ones. Other initiatives like Timeleft, 222, and Pie are similarly exploring community-driven and event-focused experiences.
[...]
“The motivation stemmed from our personal experiences. Post-college, finding travel partners becomes tougher as friends settle down, have families, or get busy,” De Nadai explained to TechCrunch. “Fabio and I considered solo travel alternatives for group experiences. While enjoyable, something felt amiss. Even though the guides were local experts, the varied age groups sometimes led to a lack of connection. Travelers were present, but not completely engaged.”
[...]
In light of this, the founders reimagined group travel to highlight shared interests. WeRoad’s trips are primarily geared towards younger travelers and revolve around specific themes and experiential categories, such as beach retreats or ski adventures.
[...]
“We pondered, ‘What if we crafted trips for Millennials and Gen Z, bringing together individuals from similar cultural backgrounds to encourage genuine connections?’” De Nadai elaborated.
[...]
Prior to each journey, participants are added to a WhatsApp group led by the trip coordinator, allowing relationships to begin forming before the trip. Group sizes generally range from eight to fifteen travelers.
[...]
“What travelers are most concerned about isn’t the destination,” De Nadai pointed out, “but whether they’ll bond with their group. To alleviate this anxiety, WeRoad meticulously designs its itineraries to foster social connectivity. More interactive or cooperative activities are typically scheduled early to enhance relationships.”
[...]
Instead of conventional tour guides, WeRoad opts for “group leaders.” These leaders are often closer in age to the travelers and accompany them throughout the entire experience. Currently, WeRoad collaborates with over 4,000 group leaders globally.
[...]
“Our aim isn’t to have specialists for every locale, but to seek individuals with travel experiences and strong interpersonal skills. Can they manage a group, resolve conflicts, adapt to changes, and connect strangers?” De Nadai remarked.
[...]
WeRoad is also broadening its offerings beyond just travel. In 2025, the company introduced WeMeet, an app dedicated to local events, featuring activities such as communal dinners, hiking, yoga sessions, running clubs, after-work meetups, and game nights. Last year, WeMeet attracted over 50,000 participants across 35 cities and achieved 150,000 downloads.
[...]
WeRoad plans to integrate WeMeet into its U.S. strategy, focusing on select cities rather than a nationwide launch, starting in Austin by recruiting group leaders, organizing local events, and building community partnerships before further expansion.
[...]
“We aspire to host WeMeet events in various U.S. cities in 2026, beginning with Austin, known for its vibrant culture and active community,” added De Nadai.
[...]
WeRoad reported €130 million in revenue for 2025, reflecting a 30% year-on-year increase and serving more than 100,000 travelers last year. Since its founding in 2017, the company has facilitated travel for over 300,000 customers across more than 1,000 itineraries worldwide.
[...]

results.title: Airbnb Leads $58 Million WeRoad Funding Round as Group Travel Startup Plans U.S. Expansion | TravelCapybara
results.author: Marcus Bennett
highlights:
Airbnb Leads $58 Million WeRoad Funding Round as Group Travel Startup Plans U.S. Expansion | TravelCapybara
[...]
Airbnb’s investment in WeRoad reflects growing demand for travel experiences built around group trips, social connection and real-world community. Photo: WeRoad
[...]
Airbnb has led a $58 million Series C funding round for WeRoad, backing the Milan-based group travel startup as it prepares for its first major expansion outside Europe. The investment brings WeRoad’s total capital raised to about $100 million and gives the company fresh resources to enter the U.S. market, starting with Austin.
[...]
Founded in 2017, WeRoad has built its business around small-group trips for younger travelers, with itineraries designed around shared interests, age groups and travel styles. The company says more than 300,000 travelers have used its platform since launch, including more than 100,000 in 2025 alone. It reported €130 million in revenue last year, up 30% year over year.
[...]
The funding round comes at a time when travel companies are increasingly focused on experiences, community and social connection rather than only bookings. WeRoad’s model is aimed at travelers who want to visit new places but are also looking for people to share the trip with. Its groups typically include eight to 15 travelers, with members connected through WhatsApp before departure so they can begin interacting before the trip starts.
[...]
The company does not use traditional tour guides in the conventional sense. Instead, it works with more than 4,000 group leaders globally, who act more like coordinators and travel companions than destination lecturers. WeRoad says those leaders are chosen for travel experience and soft skills, including the ability to manage group dynamics, adapt to changes and help strangers connect.
[...]
The deal gives Airbnb exposure to one of the fastest-growing parts of the travel market: experience-led, community-driven group travel. Airbnb has been trying to expand beyond accommodation and build a wider travel platform, including services, experiences and more social features inside its app. Investing in WeRoad gives the company a way to learn from a business that has already developed a repeatable model around group experiences.
[...]
WeRoad’s U.S. strategy will rely partly on WeMeet, its local events platform launched in 2025. The initiative focuses on curated in-person gatherings such as dinners, hikes, yoga classes, running groups, after-work drinks and board game nights. WeRoad says WeMeet has already attracted 50,000 attendees across 35 cities and reached 150,000 app downloads.
[...]
The company plans to use those events to build community before scaling its travel product in the U.S. Austin will be the first major launch market, chosen for its active community scene and energy around social experiences. Rather than expanding nationwide immediately, WeRoad plans to build city by city, recruiting group leaders, running local events and creating partnerships that can support future trips.
[...]
For Airbnb, the investment could help strengthen its long-running ambition to become more than a place to book stays. For WeRoad, it provides capital, credibility and a powerful strategic backer as it moves beyond Europe. The question now is whether its model of curated group travel can translate to the U.S. at scale.

results.title: WeRoad Secures $58 Million to Bring Its Social‑Focused Group Travel Model to the United States | Finexus
highlights:
WeRoad Secures $58 Million to Bring Its Social‑Focused Group Travel Model to the United States | Finexus
[...]
Milan’s WeRoad, a company that builds group travel experiences around shared interests rather than just destinations, announced a fresh infusion of capital that brings its total financing to roughly one hundred million dollars. The latest round, worth fifty‑eight million dollars, was led by Airbnb, which has been expanding its own portfolio of community‑centric services. The money will be deployed to launch the company’s first U.S. operations, with Austin, Texas, chosen as the initial foothold.
[...]
a broader shift in
[...]
travel and tech sectors, where the line between a booking engine and a social platform is becoming increasingly blurred. While many technology firms continue to pour resources into artificial intelligence, a growing contingent of entrepreneurs is betting on what they call the “in‑real‑life economy” – businesses that monetize face‑to‑face interaction instead of screen time. WeRoad’s model fits squarely within that trend, positioning the startup as a potential antidote to the rising sense of isolation that many younger people report.
[...]
The founders – Paolo De Nadai, Fabio Bin and Erika De Santi – first conceived the idea after experiencing the difficulty of finding travel companions once they left university and entered the workforce. “When you finish school and start a career, your friends are either having kids, moving away, or simply can’t line up their schedules,” De Nadai explained in a recent interview. The trio tried existing group‑travel services that catered to solo adventurers, but found that the itineraries felt more like guided tours than opportunities for genuine connection. Guides were often seasoned locals, while the participants spanned a wide age range, making it hard for anyone to relate to one another.
[...]
WeRoad’s answer was to redesign the travel product around common interests and demographic similarity. Each trip is curated for Millennials or members of Generation Z, grouping participants by cultural touchstones and travel preferences rather than by random assignment. Prospective travelers browse the platform for themed journeys – whether a beach‑focused escape, a ski adventure, or a culinary tour – and sign up for a group that typically numbers eight to fifteen people.
[...]
A distinctive element of the service is the pre‑trip digital community. Before departure, all participants are added to a WhatsApp chat overseen by a “group leader,” a role that replaces the traditional tour guide. These leaders are not necessarily local experts; instead, they are individuals who have traveled extensively themselves and possess strong interpersonal skills. Their job is to keep the group cohesive, defuse any tension, and help strangers become friends. De Nadai emphasized that the most common anxiety among customers is not the destination but the fear of not fitting in with the other travelers. To address this, WeRoad structures the early days of each itinerary around collaborative or adventurous activities designed to break the ice quickly.
[...]
In 2025, WeRoad broadened its scope beyond multi‑day trips with the launch of WeMeet, an app that aggregates local, in‑person gatherings such as hikes, yoga sessions, board‑game nights, and after‑work drinks. The platform reported over fifty thousand attendees across thirty‑five cities in the previous year, and it amassed roughly one hundred and fifty thousand downloads. WeMeet will serve as a cornerstone of the U.S. rollout, allowing the company to plant seeds of community before scaling its full travel offering.
[...]
Austin was selected as the launch city because of its reputation for a vibrant cultural scene and a youthful, tech‑savvy population. De Nadai explained that the plan is to first recruit a cadre of group leaders, partner with local venues, and host a series of WeMeet events to build brand awareness and trust. Once a critical mass of engaged users is established, the company intends to roll out its signature group‑travel itineraries to the city and, subsequently, to other U.S. markets throughout 2026.
[...]
The financials behind the expansion are encouraging. In 2025, WeRoad generated roughly €130 million in revenue, marking a thirty‑percent increase over the previous year, and it facilitated travel for more than one hundred thousand customers. Since its inception in 2017, the startup claims to have organized trips for over three hundred thousand people across more than a thousand distinct itineraries worldwide.
[...]
Investors appear convinced that the loneliness epidemic, especially among younger adults, represents a sizable market opportunity. The infusion from Airbnb – a company that itself has pivoted toward experiences and community building – signals confidence that a travel platform that doubles as a social network can achieve sustainable growth. Whether WeRoad can translate its European success into the competitive U.S. landscape remains to be seen, but the combination of capital, a clear community‑first product, and a phased city‑by‑city strategy gives the venture a solid foundation.
[...]
As the United States grapples with broader social challenges, including rising mental‑health concerns and a growing awareness of the costs of isolation, platforms like WeRoad may find fertile ground for expansion. If the company can replicate the sense of belonging it has cultivated among European travelers, its model could become a template for a new generation of businesses that profit by bringing people together in the physical world rather than merely connecting them online.

results.title: Meet5
results.author: Exa
highlights:
Meet5 is a Technology, Information and Internet company. meet5 is a company that allows users to connect and create meets, organize activities, and organize events. They cater to individuals in the middle age, who have a strong connection with each other. The app provides a simple and secure approach to meeting and providing a safe and enjoyable experience for their members. Meet5 employs 65 people (+64.4% YoY, +29 people), founded in 2017. Headquartered in Frankfurt, Hessen, Germany, operates in 5 countries (including Netherlands, Spain, Austria, and Israel). Has $20.4M in total funding, with 8 prior funding rounds.

Get to know like-minded people of your age and from your area in an uncomplicated way at a group meeting. Go out to eat, hike together or experience culture together - anything is possible. Meet5: You meet five new people. The app is aimed at the middle-aged generation with both feet on the ground. Meet5 is especially for those who like to try something new. Get to know each other in person without chatting for a long time beforehand. The focus of the app is on making new acquaintances, if it ever turns into something more, we're still happy! Meet5 started in Frankfurt, but we now have a strong presence throughout Germany and our first communities in Switzerland and Austria.

properties.name: MeetMux
properties.description:
MeetMux is a purpose-driven socializing platform designed to move people beyond endless scrolling and into real-world connections. We believe genuine bonds are built through shared passions, live activities, and authentic interactions.
What Makes MeetMux Different:
Verified Profiles → Real people, real connections.
Similarity Index → Find like-minded individuals instantly.
Live Activities → Discover and join what’s happening nearby in real time.
Interactive Map → See who’s around and what they’re planning.
Seamless Planning → From coffee catchups to sports, trips, and events—connect effortlessly.

Our Mission: To simplify socializing by helping people discover activities, connect meaningfully, and create memorable experiences offline.

Why MeetMux?
Unlike traditional networking or dating apps, MeetMux blends authentic engagement with real-world discovery bridging the gap between digital connections and real-life experiences.

Launching soon!

properties.name: LINK
properties.description:
LINK is social platform app that lets anybody meet anyone with whoever they’re with, for any purpose. Whether that’s making friends who like the same activities as you or finding a special someone.
We realized that people feel most comfortable meeting new people with their friends. So why is the only way to meet new people nowadays alone? There is an over emphasis on individuals nowadays and it disincentivizes group activity and spending time together. LINK combines the adventure and excitement inherent to meeting new people and discovering events with the comfort and familiarity of being with your friends. We hope LINK inspires millions to meet new people, discover amazing experiences, host events, deepen friendships, and express themselves.

We know that to achieve our ambitious goals, we’ll need to surround ourselves with as many smart, driven, and passionate people as possible. Therefore, we are actively hiring in engineering and product. If you’re interested in working on a small, fast-moving, early-stage team that is solving a tangible, world-wide problem, please don’t hesitate to reach out through our website or careers page. 

Website: https://www.link.social
Careers Page: https://linksocial.notion.site/Link-Careers-d5606e5881f24dd5884bbd38adffb6a0
