properties.name: Timeleft
properties.description:
𝗙𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲.
We exist to give the world a chance to show up again—to help people become the friend they wish they had, and through that practice, create the friendships they've been searching for.

You might know us as 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘢𝘱𝘱 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘥𝘪𝘯𝘯𝘦𝘳 𝘸𝘪𝘵𝘩 𝘴𝘵𝘳𝘢𝘯𝘨𝘦𝘳𝘴. But what we're building is 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗮𝗽𝗽—supporting the entire journey from that first brave hello to the repeated moments that turn strangers into chosen family.

We create infrastructure for real connection: ways to signal mutual interest, invite people back, and weave new faces into existing circles. We don't optimize away awkwardness or pretend friendship is effortless. We create conditions where people remember that every deep friendship started with two strangers choosing to show up—and then showing up again.

From 4 to 90+ employees in 18 months. 
150k users monthly. 
€18M ARR after 20 months. 

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁: 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗼 𝗯𝗲𝗰𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗳𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝗹 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗵𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗮𝗴𝗮𝗶𝗻, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗼 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗱 𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘂𝗽.

𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗱𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗳𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀. 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗯𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗼𝗻𝗲.

properties.name: SoMe AI
properties.description:
SoMe is building the infrastructure for real-world dating.

Dating apps have solved discovery, but failed at execution — fewer than 10% of matches ever turn into a date. The bottleneck is everything after the match: planning, coordination, and follow-through.

SoMe closes this gap by turning intent into confirmed, real-world interactions.

Our platform uses AI to guide users from matching to booking curated experiences — including restaurants, cafés, and social venues — all integrated with prepaid reservations. This removes friction, increases accountability, and significantly improves conversion from match to meeting.

We operate a two-sided marketplace:
	•	Users get personalized, end-to-end date planning
	•	Merchants receive guaranteed, high-intent, prepaid traffic

This alignment creates a fundamentally different dynamic from traditional dating apps, shifting the model from engagement-driven to outcome-driven.

We are building at the intersection of AI, consumer behavior, and local commerce — with a focus on converting online intent into offline action at scale.

results.title: Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots for companionship? The relationship between chatbot usage and social connectedness in Danish high-school students
highlights:
Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots for companionship? The relationship between chatbot usage and social connectedness in Danish high-school students - ScienceDirectSkip to main contentSkip to article
[...]
## International Journal of Human-Computer Studies
Volume 196,February 2025, 103409
International Journal of Human-Computer Studies
# Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots for companionship? The relationship between chatbot usage and social connectedness in Danish high-school students
Author links open overlay panelArthur BranHerbener,Malene FlensborgDamholdt
Show more
[...]
Add to Mendeley
Share
[...]
Cite
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhcs.2024.103409Get rights and content
Under a Creative Commonslicense
[...]
* •2.44 % of Danish high-school students engage in mutual participative conversations or seek emotional support from chatbots.
* •Students who engage in such interactions with chatbots are lonelier than their peers.
* •Students seem to be using chatbots to cope with negative feelings.
[...]
Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots to fill their social needs? The present research contributes to the ongoing scientific endeavors to understand the adoption of chatbots as social companions. Specifically, we examined how many Danish high-school students engage in friend-like conversations with chatbots, why they engage in such conversations, and whether this trend is associated with the sense of social connectedness (operationalized as loneliness and perceived social support). In pursuing this goal, a preregistered mixed-methods cross-sectional survey study was carried out. A total sample of 1599 students from 15 Danish high schools was collected. In total, 234 students (14.6 %) responded that they engaged in friend-like conversations with chatbots. Qualitative thematic analyses of free-text responses revealed two dominant ways of engaging with chatbots: utilitarian conversations (*n*= 174) and social-supportive conversations (*n*= 39). A major finding was that social-supportive chatbot users reported significantly more loneliness than non-chatbot users (*d*= 0.53) and utilitarian chatbot users (*d*= 0.52). Furthermore, social-supportive chatbot users also reported significantly less perceived social support than non-chatbot users (*d*= −0.46). Analyses also showed significant associations between higher loneliness and less perceived social support and various situational triggers for initiating conversations with chatbots, including bad mood, a need for self-disclosure, and a sense of loneliness, but not with a sense of friendship to the chatbot as a trigger of chatbot interactions. These findings suggest a trend among some socially disconnected Danish high-school students toward using chatbots to cope with negative emotions.
[...]
Conversational agent
[...]
Perceived social support
[...]
Companionship

results.title: AI companions and subjective well-being: Moderation by social connectedness and loneliness - ScienceDirect
highlights:
# AI companions and subjective well-being: Moderation by social connectedness and loneliness
[...]
•

Lonelier individuals showed more positive associations with AI companions.
[...]
Artificial Intelligence (AI) companions—such as Replika (Official-site-d), Character.AI (Official-site-b-b), Anima AI (Official-site-a-a), and Cotomo (Official-site-c) —have become increasingly prevalent worldwide. Designed to offer friendship, emotional support, or even romantic companionship, these conversational agents have rapidly proliferated in recent years. For instance, Replika alone is estimated to have surpassed 25 million users as of 2023 (Maples et al., 2024). Human–AI Interaction is poised to assume a substantial role in daily communication.
[...]
Moreover, the impact of AI companions is likely to be heterogeneous. Not all individuals may benefit equally; for some, such interactions could even be detrimental. Investigating such heterogeneity is essential to address ethical and safety considerations. As AI companions provide forms of social support, their benefits may be amplified among individuals who lack these resources in real life. In particular, social network size, the availability of social support, and perceived loneliness are expected to moderate the association between AI companion use and well-being. Individuals with fewer real-life connections or higher levels of loneliness may derive greater psychological benefit from AI companionship. However, to our knowledge, no empirical studies have systematically examined how these social factors moderate the well-being effects of AI companion use.
[...]
Accordingly, the primary aim of this study is to examine the association between AI companion use and well-being, with particular focus on how social networks, social support, and loneliness moderate this relationship. Specifically, we investigate whether improvements in well-being associated with AI companions are more pronounced among individuals experiencing limited social connection and feeling greater loneliness. By focusing on those who stand to benefit most from supplemental forms of social support, we aim to clarify whether AI companions can serve as a meaningful resource for enhancing well-being.
[...]
H2-1
[...]
Loneliness is the perception of social isolation or the subjective feeling of being lonely (National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, 2020). It is the distress experienced when there is an emotional gap between desired and actual social relationships (General, 2023). A large body of research has shown that higher loneliness is consistently associated with poorer emotional health and lower levels of well-being (Hawkley & Cacioppo, 2010; Holt-Lunstad, 2022). Existing research reveals that individuals who experience elevated levels of loneliness may seek AI companions to fulfil unmet emotional needs (Maples et al., 2024; Phang et al., 2025). In such cases, AI companions may function as surrogate social partners, offering emotional connection and psychological support that help mitigate the adverse effects of loneliness. Therefore, we propose the following hypothesis.
[...]
For the purpose of this study, participants who selected the item “Interaction with AI-based companion applications (e.g., Replika, Character.AI, Anima AI, Cotomo)” were classified as companion AI users (n = 291 out of 3219 AI users). These applications are designed primarily for social-oriented communication and companionship rather than task-oriented or informational functions (Bayor et al., 2025). Those who used generative AI for other listed purposes, but not for AI companionship, were classified as non-companion AI users (n = 2928 out of 3219 AI users). Participants who used both companion and non-companion functions were categorized as companion AI users to reflect the presence of companionship-oriented use.
[...]
Psychological distress was more frequently reported by companion AI users (24.7 %) compared to non-users (9.7 %). While family-based social network and support levels were similar across groups, AI users—particularly companion AI users—reported significantly higher levels of friend-based social network and support (p < 0.001). Additionally, companion AI users exhibited greater loneliness (mean score = 6.22) than non-users (mean = 5.60).
[...]
### 4.4. Moderation by loneliness (H3)
[...]
Fig. 4 illustrates the interaction between AI use and loneliness scores across four measures of well-being. Companion AI use showed a clear positive gradient across all well-being outcomes as loneliness increased. The associations with life satisfaction, happiness, purpose in life, and meaning of life all strengthened at higher levels of loneliness. Notably, the effect estimates became larger and statistically significant in the upper range of loneliness scores, suggesting that individuals experiencing greater loneliness may derive substantial psychological benefits from companion AI interactions. These findings support H3.
[...]
Predicted mean levels of each outcome across loneliness scores for non-users, non-companion AI users, and companion AI users are shown in Supplementary Fig. 3. Across all three groups, higher loneliness was associated with lower predicted levels of life satisfaction, happiness, purpose in life, and meaning of life, consistent with the well-established negative link between loneliness and well-being. However, the curves for companion AI users tended to lie above those for non-users and non-companion AI users—particularly at higher loneliness scores—suggesting that the association between companion AI use and higher well-being is most pronounced among lonelier individuals.
[...]
In this large online panel survey of Japanese adults, the use of conversational AI companions—but not other forms of AI—was associated with higher levels of life satisfaction, happiness, and purpose in life, supporting our first hypothesis (H1). Beyond average effect estimate, our study contributes to the growing literature by examining the moderating roles of social network/support and loneliness. Specifically, the benefits of AI companionship may be most pronounced among individuals with moderate levels of friend-based social network/support (H2-1) and those experiencing the highest levels of loneliness (H3). Consistent with this, supplementary spline-based analyses of predicted outcome levels suggested that companion AI use primarily alters the shape of the friend–well-being and loneliness–well-being associations, rather than simply shifting their overall levels, when compared with non-users and non-companion AI users. Although the survey design is broadly analogous to major national panel studies in other countries, to our knowledge few existing surveys currently include dedicated measures of companion AI use together with multi-domain well-being and validated social network and loneliness scales; thus, our findings should be regarded as an initial contribution from the Japanese context.
[...]
Companion-oriented conversational AI is positively associated with multiple dimensions of well-being, with the strongest associations observed among individuals with moderate levels of friend-based social network/support and those experiencing pronounced loneliness. These findings suggest that AI companions may be most effective when they both provide emotional satisfaction and support ongoing social engagement, thereby contributing to the promotion of well-being. Our results highlight the promise of AI companions as a digital tool for psychosocial support, particularly for socially vulnerable populations, where they may help reduce disparities in emotional well-being. However, to fully realize this potential, longitudinal and experimental studies are essential—to establish causal relationships, optimize design features, and identify strategies to mitigate potential unintended harms, such as over-reliance or social withdrawal.

results.title: Emerging AI individualism: how young people integrate social AI into everyday life | Communication and Change | Springer Nature Link
highlights:
Young people are increasingly growing up in a social environment with artificial intelligence (AI) as it is taken up, e.g., in education (Ali et al., 2024; Sidoti, Park, & Gottfried, 2025; Williams et al., 2024), mental health (Maples et al. 2024), entertainment, and social platforms (Hasse et al., 2019). Such uptake of AI may reshape how people seek support, express emotion, and form connections (Boyd & Holton, 2018), potentially leading to changes in the structure of social life (Brandtzaeg, Skjuve & Følstad, 2025). This social integration of AI is largely enabled by social AI, defined as “intelligent systems that are made to interact socially with human beings” (Sætra, 2020 p. 310). Examples range from large language models like ChatGPT and Gemini to AI companions such as Replika, Character.AI, and Snapchat’s My AI.
[...]
To address this empirical and conceptual gap, we apply the emerging concept of AI individualism (Brandtzaeg et al., 2025) to examine how youth increasingly navigate social life through AI companionship and guidance. This study contributes to the development of this framework by grounding it in lived user experience, reflecting both the empowering and troubling dimensions of this shift. We explore this through the following research questions:
[...]
Support and Companionship: People create close relationships with their social AI, which can provide a new form of non-human social support. Social support can be divided into four subcategories: instrumental (e.g., help and assistance with a problem), informational (e.g., information that enables the human to solve a problem), emotional (e.g.., empathy and care), or appraisal support (e.g., feedback and advice related to personal problems and development) (Langford et al., 1997). However, such companionship risks fostering emotional dependency on systems that simulate care without consciousness or accountability, potentially weakening users’ capacity for reciprocal human relationships
[...]
This restructuring of social life is not limited to broader network dynamics; it also reshapes how individuals experience intimacy and emotional support. Social AI increasingly enables users to fulfill their closer social and emotional needs through technology, thereby reducing reliance on human relationships. For example, social AI can function as a romantic partner (Skjuve et al., 2021), with some users forming lasting relationships that they describe in deeply relational terms. For instance, users of the social AI Replika a prominent example, often refer to their AI as a"friend,""long-distance girlfriend or partner,"or a part of a"mutual relationship"(Brandtzaeg et al., 2022; Skjuve et al., 2021), indicating new complex forms of personal and intimate relationships with social AI-technologies. In these scenarios, social AI provides on-demand support for humans, which may also challenge us to understand the real value of machines versus humans when it comes to needs for social support and relationships.
[...]
How social AIs like ChatGPT and My AI are used among young people has mainly been explored in the context of university students (Abdaljaleel et al., 2024). Here, students have reported perceived benefits of the technology, such as personalized learning (Firat, 2023), efficiency and ease of use (Ngo, 2023), and the ability of the technology to support comprehension (Singh et al., 2023). Research has also suggested a positive effect on critical, creative, and reflexive thinking, at least in the short term (Essel et al., 2024). However, studies of the same target group have also addressed issues in using social AI, such as inaccuracies in the information provided (Essel et al., 2024), plagiarism and wrongful usage, and potential negative impact on cognitive abilities (Singh et al., 2023). A recent study of Danish high-schoolers use of social AI found that 14% of the sample use social AI, with a small portion of the participants (2,5%) using it for social support purposes. Furthermore, this study found that students engaging in such use reported higher scores on loneliness and also to use the social AI for coping with negative feelings (Herbener & Damholdt, 2025). These findings suggest that while social AI remains a niche form of interaction among youth, it may serve as an emotional support mechanism for those experiencing social or psychological vulnerability. This raises important questions about whether such interactions complement or substitute for human social contact in emotionally significant ways.
[...]
In sum, existing research indicates a trend towards AI individualism, also amongst youth. Studies show that young individuals utilize social AI for education, learning, and gaining more independence in their social interactions. While social AI may enhance autonomy and emotional support, it also risks fostering dependency and substituting human relationships with machine-mediated interactions. Furthermore, the use of AI as a coping mechanism for loneliness or distress raises ethical concerns about the long-term effects on mental health. However, there is still a gap in understanding how young people integrate social AI into their daily lives and how they experience such use.
[...]
Some participants experienced the emotional and social support provided by social AI. Some appreciated its role as a non-judgmental, empathetic friend always available for a chat. Others valued the anonymity it offers, as indicated by the participant below.
[...]
Our findings also suggest that some young people actively make use of social AI in cognitive and creative processes. This indicates that social AI might increasingly be viewed as co-creators, playing a significant role in educational and personal contexts, such as leisure and entertainment. Hence, our findings partly mirror a recent study by Maples et al. (2024) who found that the social AI Replika is utilized by students in multiple intersecting roles—including that of a friend, therapist, and for intellectual reflection. As such, the findings may suggest a future where social AI facilitates a deeper engagement with topics and tasks integral to AI individualism. However, such use may also displace opportunities for peer collaboration as discussed above.
[...]
This study offers one of the first empirical accounts of how young people integrate social AI into their everyday lives, using the emerging concept of AI individualism to understand shifts in sociality and access to social capital. While adoption is still emerging, a growing subset of youth increasingly turn to social AI like ChatGPT and MyAI for personalized interaction, support, and advice. The findings of our study contribute novel insights into how social AI may impact not just communication patterns but also emotional and cognitive dependencies. Our findings raise concerns about overreliance, erosion of peer-based support, and the potential displacement of human relationships – in particular weak tie relationships. As social AI becomes more embedded in youth culture, it is vital that researchers, designers, and policymakers attend not only to its empowering affordances, but also to the negative social implications it may hold. In doing so, the study advances the conceptual development of AI individualism by grounding it in users’ reported lived experiences and relational practices with social AI.
[...]
Herbener, A. B., & Damholdt, M. F. (2025). Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots for companionship? The relationship between chatbot usage and social connectedness in Danish high-school students. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 196, Article 103409. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhcs.2024.103409
[...]
Maples, B., Cerit, M., Vishwanath, A., & Pea, R. (2024). Loneliness and suicide mitigation for students using GPT3-enabled chatbots. npj Mental Health Research, 3(1), 4. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44184-023-00047-6
[...]
